_meta.purpose: Single source of truth for pages/support/ai-policy.html. Drives a single-card / mixed-flow layout via render_ai_policy.py. Voice adapted from IS3513 GenAI Policy with programming-context examples.

intro_html: AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and GitHub Copilot exist. You’ll probably use them throughout your career. The question for this course isn’t “can you use AI?” — it’s what role AI plays in your learning.

openingCallout.title: Are you here to learn this material, or are you here for a grade?
openingCallout.body_html: Pick honestly. The rest of this policy depends on your answer.

framing_html:
If you’re here to learn, AI is one of the most powerful tools you’ll ever have. Used right, it’s a force-multiplier — it accelerates your understanding, sharpens your code, and helps you grow into a programmer who can collaborate with AI fluently. You’ll leave this course better at Python AND better at working with AI. Both, at the same time.

If you’re here just for a grade, AI can technically do most of this course for you. The catch is that we both know how that ends. You’ll get the grade. You won’t have the skills. And when a recruiter asks you to write a small Python function on a whiteboard, or to walk through code you “wrote” for IS2053, you’ll be staring at a screen you’ve never actually used. That’s the moment that decides whether you get a programming career or not — and AI can’t be in the room with you.

decisionCallout.title: The Question That Decides Everything
decisionCallout.body_html:
Before you submit any work, ask yourself one question: “Will I be able to do this work at my first job — without asking AI for help?”

If the answer is yes, you’ve used AI the right way. You learned something, validated it against your own knowledge, and produced code you can defend.

If the answer is no, you probably won’t get that first job — and if you do, you won’t keep it. Interviews are how the industry filters out people who can’t do the work. AI can write you a great résumé. It can’t make you good at the live coding screen.

whyEmployersCare.label: Why Employers Care
whyEmployersCare.body_html:
Before you even get to the job, you have to get through the interview. Software engineering interviews include live coding screens — whiteboard problems, take-home coding exercises, pair-programming sessions. Recruiters specifically design these to filter out candidates who can talk about code but can’t write it. You can’t bring ChatGPT to a Zoom interview where the panel is watching your screen.

When you walk into your first programming job, your manager isn’t going to grade your work on a curve. They’re going to hand you a real problem — a bug in production, a feature to ship, a deadline that matters — and expect you to know what to do. They’ll assume you actually learned what your transcript says you learned.

The skills this course builds — writing clean Python, debugging your own code, reading documentation, breaking problems into steps — are exactly what employers and certifications test for. AI accelerates the learning; it doesn’t replace it.

items.title: Explaining Python syntax
items.desc: Ask what a function or operator does, then use it yourself

items.title: Troubleshooting errors
items.desc: Paste the error, get debugging help, verify the fix works

items.title: Understanding concepts
items.desc: Have AI explain an idea a different way until it clicks

items.title: Proofreading & grammar
items.desc: Refine your own docstrings and comments for clarity

items.title: Suggesting structure
items.desc: Get feedback on how to organize code you’ve already written

items.title: Generating code you didn’t write
items.desc: The code itself must be yours, line by line

items.title: Answering quiz questions
items.desc: Quizzes verify YOU did the hands-on work

items.title: Completing BookEx exercises
items.desc: The book exercises are calibration for your own skill

items.title: Fabricating output or screenshots
items.desc: If you didn’t run it, don’t claim you did

items.title: Substituting AI predictions for running code
items.desc: If AI tells you what a script would output, that doesn’t replace running it

ok.label: ✓ OK to Ask
items_html: “Why am I getting an IndentationError on line 14?”
“How should I structure my function to handle both cases?” (you’ve written the code; you’re asking how to refine it)
“Is this docstring clear?” (you wrote it; AI helps you refine it)

notOk.label: ✗ Not OK to Ask
items_html: “Write a Python program that reads a CSV and prints averages” (the coding is your work product)
“Solve this lab for me” (the coding is what you’re being assessed on)
“What would this script print if I ran it?” (the answer is “go run it and find out” — predicting tool output is how you avoid building intuition)

tutorCallout.title: Tutor, Not Ghostwriter
tutorCallout.body_html:
Think of AI like a tutor — someone who explains, demonstrates, and helps you think clearly. Not a ghostwriter who hands you finished code to put your name on.

Tutors make you smarter. Ghostwriters make you fragile.

subSections.heading: AI multiplies what you already know. It doesn’t replace what you don’t.
subSections.body_html:
If you understand the topic, AI accelerates you. If you don’t, AI gives you confident-sounding code you can’t evaluate, defend, or fix when it’s wrong. Zero times anything is still zero.

A student who can’t explain what a for loop does will not become a programmer by asking ChatGPT to write the lab. They’ll just have code they can’t defend on a quiz, in office hours when I ask “walk me through line 14,” or in a job interview when someone asks them to modify it.

subSections.heading: The most transferable skill you’ll build this semester is learning how to ask better questions.
subSections.body_html:
The students who get the most out of AI tools are the ones who already have the vocabulary to ask precisely. “Fix my code” is a beginner prompt. “This Python script throws a KeyError on line 14 when the input dict is missing the ‘status’ field — should I use .get() with a default, or catch the exception?” is a professional prompt. The difference isn’t the AI — it’s the human knowing enough domain vocabulary to frame the problem.

That’s why the readings, the BookEx exercises, and the hands-on lab work matter. They build the vocabulary that makes AI actually useful to you. Skip the work, skip the vocabulary, and AI becomes a crutch that hides what you don’t know — right up until the moment someone asks you to explain it.

subSections.heading: Validate everything.
subSections.body_html: AI confidently produces wrong answers. Hallucinated method names, deprecated syntax, wrong indentation, code that imports modules that don’t exist, examples that look right but throw runtime errors. If you can’t independently verify what it gave you — by running it, reading the official Python docs, or checking against the textbook — you can’t use it. “The AI told me” is not a defense. In a professional setting, your name goes on the deliverable, not the model’s.

scopeCompliance.label: Scope Compliance
scopeCompliance.body_html:
CodeGrade uses Scope Compliance checking. It automatically detects if your code uses techniques we haven’t covered yet.

AI-generated code almost always uses advanced features, libraries, or patterns that students in an intro course wouldn’t know yet. When we see list comprehensions in Module 1, or try/except in Module 2, or regex in Module 3… we know.

warningCallout.title: Consequence
warningCallout.body_html: Scope violations result in an automatic zero on that test. Using techniques you haven’t learned is clear evidence of outside help.

honestTest.label: The Honest Test
honestTest.body_html:
If I asked you to walk me through the code you submitted, line by line, could you do it without your AI tab open?

If the answer is no, the work isn’t yours yet. Go back and learn it before you submit.

The goal of this course is to make you a programmer, not to make you good at prompting AI. You’ll have plenty of time to use AI tools later — but first, you need to understand what the AI is actually doing. Build the foundation now. AI will be more powerful in your hands for the rest of your career if you do.

bottomLineCallout.title: The Bottom Line
bottomLineCallout.body_html:
The goal of this course is to make you a programmer with a working AI methodology — both at the same time.

If you can defend the code as yours, AI helped you. If you can’t, it replaced you.

footer_html: Questions about what’s allowed? Ask in Discord, or email.
